title: How to safely inject a previously broken nose
filename: How to safely inject a previously broken nose.en.vtt
excerpt: Today I want to share with you an interesting case this is a patient who's been known to me for a very long time and she had a very interesting backstory to her non-surgical rhinoplasty a broken nose surgery that did not fix the problem and the important role that non-surgical rh…

segments.text: today I want to share with you an interesting case this is a patient who's been known to me for a very long time and she had a very interesting backstory to her non-surgical rhinoplasty a broken nose surgery that did not fix the problem and the important role that non-surgical rhinoplasty is played in her life she had this first treatment just before she got married it's lasted a very long time and today she came back for a Top-Up we had a little bit about her story and there's a little bit here that will help you if you're concerned about treating patients who've had previous surgery or previous injuries to the nose we discussed some of this in this case you'll see me bend the tip of a needle which I've covered on a separate YouTube video that you can watch here and in this case you can see how it makes a little bit easier for me to access the right depth and position and potentially to increase the safety of injection through the needle touching the periosteum bent in such a way as the bevel is down to decrease the potential space where you may be able to get into a vessel I know it's often said that you should never treat a patient who has had a surgical rhinoplas before

now what's the reason behind this it's all to do with the tightness of the collagen around the nose and the potential space that you have to expand any tissue and also potentially there's actually less blood flow to the nose anyway because sometimes they tie off for example a columnar artery is sometimes tied off during a surgical rhinoplasty so there may be less blood flow to the skin and also more pressure because of more connective tissue so how come I feel safe enough to treat this patient well the first thing I do with any non-surgical rhinoplasty is feel for any adhesions if you have a nose that's very tightly stuck down to the surface of the bone or the periosteum or the cartilage then you have much less room to inject this is a complete contraindication if you do inject a nose like this what you'll see is a white bleb that arises when you inject and that's because the pressure of the filler is now squeezing blood out of the capillaries instead of lifting the skin off the periosteum now you can usually massage this away if you pick it up immediately but I would not inject any further now in this case I've already tested her nose I've known her for many years I know there's space here and I'm

injecting small amounts and checking completely refill after every injection and with this combined often with an ultrasound to validate blood flow or to at least know where the blood vessels are you can inject with more confidence but as I always say there are no five minute nose jobs 55 minutes is my style of nose job small injections one at a time loss of checking lots of pausing in between validating blood flow and you should be safe even from the side here it's kind of not perfectly straight but it is straight it's not a not an indentation yet so I kind of think of this as a tidying up yeah so I put a little bend on the needle so that I can approach it from a different angle now in terms of blood vessels here we have lateral nasal artery that curls up dorsal nasal artery and because we're off the midline with where this indentation is I will be near those vessels so I'm going to do very small amounts each time and aspirate to check I'm looking also

very carefully at the reflection I don't know if you'll see it on the camera but if you rotate your head left and right often you reveal the Contours of the nose with more detail it gets complicated in terms of the reflections around here whereas this side it seems a bit simpler and this is the kind of thing that patients see when they're putting foundation on you trying to smooth the complexity of the nose and you'll notice it more and when this is when this job is done that'll become a much easier simpler task but I often only see it when either I move my head or where I move the patient's head to see the light reflex as it moves over the surface if you imagine the moon when it's just catching half like a crescent moon you'll see the details of the craters much more easily just as the light is coming from the side and that's what I'm doing by rotating around is seeing the surface with more detail before I inject I can spray

I also want to see the tissue rise as the filler goes in which I can see so now I see the reflection comes up here and then there's a little indentation right here then the reflection continues so this is a midline injection I'll do one right in the middle line I was right where the cartilages actually adjacent to the Bone so I'm not going to be able to go all the way deep I don't want to inject that deep if you're wondering what product I'm using in this procedure it is juvenile and voluma I am not a representative of Allegan but I do like this product for its ability to hold its shape over time as exemplified by this patient who had

four years between treatments thank you [Music] I hope you found that helpful if you want to learn more on the topic on how to inject the nose more safely make sure you watch this video here on nose filler safety if you want to learn more about how and when to bend a needle and how to do it safely without getting your needle tip dirty click on this video here [Music]
